name: Market Sizing
blurb: Estimate the size of a real market in 15 minutes.
prompt: Estimate the annual revenue (in INR) of the electric two-wheeler market in India for 2026. Show your assumptions step-by-step, then state your final number with one sentence on the biggest source of uncertainty.
rubric: Structure: top-down or bottom-up explicitly stated
Assumptions: 4+ named with rough numbers
Arithmetic: final number is consistent with assumptions
Sensitivity: identifies the biggest uncertainty

name: Guesstimate
blurb: Crack an offbeat estimation under pressure.
prompt: How many cups of chai are sold in Mumbai on a single weekday? Walk through your logic, assumptions, and the final number.
rubric: Population segmentation logic is explicit
Per-capita consumption assumption stated
Reasonable arithmetic
Honest about overestimates/underestimates

name: PRD Writing
blurb: Write a focused 1-page PRD for a real user problem.
prompt: A FinTech app for college students sees 60% of new users abandon during KYC. Write a 1-page PRD for the fix. Include: problem statement, user persona, success metric, prioritized solution (3 bullets), what you'd cut.
rubric: Problem statement is sharp and quantified
Success metric is measurable and time-bound
Solution is prioritized — clear what comes first
Explicitly names what's NOT in scope

name: 3-Statement Model Logic
blurb: Show you understand how P&L, BS, CF link.
prompt: A company books ₹100Cr of revenue on credit (90-day terms). Working through it: (1) what changes in P&L this quarter, (2) what changes in the balance sheet, (3) what changes in cash flow statement, (4) what changes next quarter when cash is collected. Be specific about line items.
rubric: Correctly identifies revenue recognition in P&L this quarter
Identifies AR build on balance sheet
Correctly handles cash flow (operating activities adjustment)
Next-quarter reversal is right

name: Positioning Brief
blurb: Position a startup against an entrenched giant.
prompt: You're launching a B2B accounting SaaS in India against Tally and Zoho Books. Write a one-paragraph positioning statement (March, Geoff Moore style): For [target], who [need], our product is [category] that [unique benefit], unlike [competitor], we [differentiator]. Then add 2 sentences on why this differentiator is defensible.
rubric: Follows positioning template structure
Target customer is narrow and specific
Differentiator is genuinely different, not a feature list
Defensibility argument holds up

name: Competitor Teardown
blurb: Read a competitor's strategy from public signals.
prompt: Pick Zomato's most recent product move you remember (e.g. Zomato Go, district, blinkit integration). In 5 short bullets: (1) what they shipped, (2) the business problem they were solving, (3) the implicit assumption they're betting on, (4) the biggest risk to that bet, (5) what you'd watch over 6 months to know if it worked.
rubric: Picks a real and recent move
Business problem is named, not feature description
Implicit bet is non-obvious
Risk is concrete and falsifiable
Leading indicator is measurable
